entry.rationale: This is the core question the project was created to answer. A marriage record — whether civil registration, church register, or bond/license — would name the bride under her maiden name and record the date and place of the ceremony. The two unknowns (maiden name and marriage event) are both attributes of the same record type, making this a single testable question. No prior research exists on this project, so this is the necessary first question before any decomposition into ancillary lines.

entry.rationale: FamilySearch 'England Marriages, 1538-1973' (collection 1473015) is the broadest indexed pre-civil-registration England marriage database. Thomas Young married Elizabeth (unknown surname) circa 1828 in Gloucestershire. Searching this collection with name variants should surface the marriage record and reveal the bride's maiden name and the parish where they married. Free and indexed — highest-probability first search.

FamilySearch 'England, Births and Christenings, 1538-1975' (collection 1473014) indexes christening/baptism entries. Several Young children were christened at Bitton St Mary the Virgin 1830-1841. Some pre-civil-registration baptism registers record both parents' full names including the mother's maiden name in the entry or margin. Reviewing these entries may reveal Elizabeth's maiden name indirectly even without a direct marriage record.

England & Wales civil birth registration began July 1837. Thomas and Elizabeth had at least seven children born after that date (John Apr 1841, Robert Feb 1843, Evan c.1845, Ellen c.1848, James Oct 1853, William Apr 1857). A civil birth certificate for any of these children records the mother's full name including maiden name ('Elizabeth Young formerly [maiden name]'). The FamilySearch 'England and Wales, Birth Registration Index, 1837-2008' (collection 2285338) provides the reference needed to order the certificate or access the full image via Findmypast/Ancestry. This is one of the most reliable routes to Elizabeth's maiden name.

FamilySearch 'England, Gloucestershire, Non-Conformist Church Records, 1581-1997' (collection 3648679) covers Methodist, Baptist, and other Dissenting registers for the county. The Bitton/Oldland/Kingswood area of Gloucestershire was a stronghold of Methodism from Wesley's era. If Thomas or Elizabeth's family were non-conformist, the marriage record may appear here rather than in a Church of England register.

FindMyPast holds comprehensive Gloucestershire Church of England parish register transcripts including pre-1837 marriage registers for Bitton and adjacent parishes (Oldland, Keynsham, Warmley, Hanham). The FamilySearch volume search shows the Bitton marriage register on FamilySearch only begins July 1837, so the 1828 marriage register is not on FamilySearch — FindMyPast's Gloucestershire transcripts are the primary paid resource for accessing it. Search for Thomas Young marriages in Gloucestershire 1820-1835 with surname variants.

Thomas and Elizabeth Young appear in the 1841 census at Walcot, Bath (known from tree data). The 1841 census does not record relationships or maiden names, but it confirms the couple's co-residence, gives approximate ages (helpful to verify identity), records the birthplace 'Y' or 'N' (born in the county), and lists any children still at home. It provides context for interpreting any marriage record found and helps distinguish this Thomas Young from others in Gloucestershire. Also note: any children listed may narrow the marriage window.

If indexed searches on FamilySearch and FindMyPast fail, the pre-1837 Bitton St Mary the Virgin marriage register (not digitized on FamilySearch) should be browsed directly. It is held at Gloucestershire Archives (Gloucester). The register covers the Hardwicke Act period (1754-1837) in the standard printed banns/licence format which names both parties with their parishes of residence. This item uses the search-images skill to locate the volume on FamilySearch if a microfilm copy exists, or directs to Gloucestershire Archives online catalogue.

entry.description: Birth year conflict for p-child-elizabeth: burial register (src_001) records birth ~1829 (secondary, family recollection at time of burial); Bitton christening record in tree records birth/christening 1832. Three-year discrepancy.
entry.resolution: The burial register's ~1829 birth year is secondary information recorded at burial, relying on family recollection. The Bitton christening date of 10 Jun 1832 is primary evidence recorded at the time of the sacrament. In pre-1837 England, civil registration did not exist and delayed christenings were common; a child born ~1829-1832 and christened only in June 1832 is historically plausible. The tree's 'birth: 1832' was derived from the christening date, not an independent birth record. The burial register's estimate of ~1829 cannot be confirmed or refuted without a direct birth record. Adopted position: the Bitton christening (10 Jun 1832) is primary and the burial register estimate (~1829) is secondary; the child's birth year remains uncertain (range 1829-1832), with 1832 preferred as primary evidence. This conflict does not affect q_001.
entry.weighing_analysis: Secondary burial-register estimate (~1829) vs. primary christening record (1832). The conflict is explained by the difference in information quality: the register clerk at the 1833 burial relied on family-supplied recollection for the birth year, while the Bitton christening record was contemporaneous. Delayed christening accounts for the apparent gap. No independent birth record exists to adjudicate; the primary source (christening) is preferred.

entry.description: Birthplace conflict for p-thomas: 1841 census (src_002) indexes birthplace as 'Somerset, England' (secondary); tree places birth at Bitton, Gloucestershire. The 1841 census birth column records Y/N for born-in-same-county-as-enumerated, not a free-text birthplace.
entry.resolution: The 1841 census birth column records only a Y/N/F indicator (born in same county as enumerated = Y; different county = N; foreign = F). The FamilySearch indexer converted this to 'Somerset' (the county of enumeration), which incorrectly implies Somerset as birthplace. Since p-thomas was enumerated in Walcot, Somerset, and was born in Gloucestershire, the correct census entry would be N (not born in Somerset), and the 'Somerset' birthplace in the index is an indexer interpretation artifact. The tree's Bitton, Gloucestershire birth is based on the 7 Aug 1809 Bitton christening entry (confirmed in log_004 search, top result). The Bitton/Gloucestershire birth is primary; the census entry is secondary and of indeterminate quality for birthplace.
entry.weighing_analysis: The 1841 census is a derivative source for birthplace — it records only a binary county indicator, not a free-text birthplace. The FamilySearch index's 'Somerset' is a misinterpretation of the Y/N column. The Bitton, Gloucestershire birth (from the 1809 Bitton christening record, top hit in log_004) is primary. Conflict resolved in favor of Bitton, Gloucestershire. No further action needed for q_001.

entry.rationale: Thomas and Elizabeth Young had at least one child (Elizabeth, buried April 1833). A search of FamilySearch England Births/Christenings 1538-1975 for children with father Thomas Young in Gloucestershire/Somerset could surface baptism records listing the mother. Non-Conformist christening registers in particular sometimes recorded the mother's maiden name or pre-nuptial surname alongside the father's. Any record naming 'Elizabeth (née X) wife of Thomas Young' would directly answer the maiden-name question. This is the highest-probability remaining FamilySearch avenue since the marriage register itself is not digitized.

The 1841 census found Thomas Young at Walcot, Bath without his wife. The 1851 census would show Thomas (age ~42) with his household. If Elizabeth was still alive, she would appear and her specific birthplace would narrow the search for her pre-marriage baptism record, which is key to finding her maiden name. The 1851 census also records birthplace more precisely than the 1841 (county level), which could confirm whether Elizabeth was born in Gloucestershire or across the border in Somerset.

The child Elizabeth Young was buried in a Non-Conformist burial ground (collection 3648679). The parents Thomas and Elizabeth were likely registered members of the same congregation. A direct search for 'Thomas Young' and 'Elizabeth Young' as principals (not as parents of a deceased child) in the Non-Conformist and Non-Parochial Registers collection may surface membership entries, baptism-of-adults records, or marriage entries for this congregation. Non-Conformist bodies often recorded fuller biographical details including maiden names at membership.
